key_insight: Swift heavy ion tracks in Al2O3 are shaped by a radius-dependent collapse of the electronic band gap, with full metallization near the ion path and partial gap shrinkage farther out.
engineering_relevance: Track-damage models for irradiated ceramics should account for radial electronic heterogeneity, since it changes how much electronic excitation is converted into localized atomic heating and discontinuous damage.
method: TREKIS Monte Carlo simulates the initial electronic and ionic excitation after ion passage.
DFT-based molecular dynamics tracks transient band-structure changes inside the ion track.
The coupled model maps radial band-gap collapse to lattice heating and defect formation.
evidence_signal: For a 700 MeV Bi ion, the abstract reports metallization within about 2 nm of the trajectory, band-gap shrinkage at roughly 3-5 nm, and little change beyond 5 nm.
watch_item: The heating and damage estimates are model-dependent and rely on accurate coupling between the electronic excitation simulation and the DFT-based band-structure response.
paper_title: Effect of radially heterogeneous band gap collapse on formation of swift heavy ion tracks in Al2O3
